items.title: Deniz Undav: Germany's 2026 World Cup supersub

items.title: Re-awakening the Nigerian conscience against corruption, By Babafemi Badejo
items.description: Introduction Corruption in Nigeria has gone beyond episodic or high-profile malfeasance to become a systemic, normalised, and almost ritualised feature of public and private life. Despite a plethora of anti-corruption agencies, legislative instruments, international treaties, and rhetorical commitments from successive administrations, the scourge of corruption persists with remarkable resilience. This paper argues that the failure […] The post Re-awakening the Nigerian conscience against corruption, By Babafemi Badejo appeared first on Premium Times Nigeria .

Dozens of ships head through Strait of Hormuz after US-Iran deal

items.title: Alan Greenspan: champion of neoliberal finance
items.description: For nearly two decades, Alan Greenspan was the most influential central banker on the planet. His death invites a fresh look at the ideas that transformed, and shook the global economy

items.title: Wanted: Jobs, critical minerals as Zambia's election nears
items.description: Zambians head to the polls in August with jobs and living costs at the top of their concerns. The outcome could also impact the ongoing global competition for critical minerals and redefine ties with China and the US.

items.title: Dettol apologises after ad to clean up 'toxic men' backfires in China
items.description: The ad sparked accusations of sexism in China, after featuring a man looking for a partner who is "not tainted by other men".

items.title: EU hosts Taliban for migrant return talks in Brussels
items.description: The EU is hosting Taliban officials after Belgium granted temporary visas for talks on returning failed Afghan asylum-seekers. The meeting is one of the bloc's highest-level contacts with the Taliban since 2021.

items.title: Right sweeps Latin America with new victory in Colombia
items.description: The right has won elections from Argentina to Honduras. But experts see little evidence of a structural ideological shift. Instead, they point to a political landscape that favours anti-establishment outsider candidates. Leer más

items.title: France issues red alert as heatwave breaks records and death toll mounts
items.description: France experienced its hottest-ever night Monday into Tuesday as Prime Minister Sébastien Lecornu said 40 people had drowned across the country since June 18 as they sought to escape the record heat. In a country without widespread air-conditioning, the scorching temperatures have led to school closures, cancelled events and disrupted rail services.

items.title: How 100 hospitals switched to pen and paper to defeat a national cyber-attack
items.description: For four days, dozens of Romanian hospitals went offline, as cyber-experts sought to defeat the hackers.

items.title: Spanish PM faces mounting pressure as party mired in political scandals
items.description: Spanish Prime Minister Pedro Sanchez said on June 23 that his government would continue its work, despite mounting political pressure following the corruption conviction of his former transport minister a day earlier. His ⁠ruling Socialist Workers' Party ​faces several court cases involving allegations of graft. Prime Minister Pedro Sanchez has not been named in ​any of the cases to date, but more than a dozen people, including his wife and brother and an influential former Socialist premier have been implicated.

items.title: Daily Quiz | On history of the treaties and territory of Antarctica
items.description: The Antarctic Treaty System, which came into force on June 23, 1961, remains one of the most ambitious international frameworks governing an entire continent. How well do you know the history of the treaties and territory of Antarctica?

items.title: World Cup drives Brazil's online betting boom
items.description: With the World Cup underway, Brazil is experiencing an online betting boom, as over a third of adults are expected to bet on the tournament. The boom comes with consequences, as official figures show a link with mental health issues. A bill is even going through the Brazilian parliament to ban celebrities from advertising for betting companies. FRANCE 24's Jan Onoszko tells us more from Rio de Janeiro.

items.title: Experts fired by Trump resurrect mothballed climate website
items.description: Fired US federal workers have revived a defunct climate website — pushing back as the Trump administration escalates cuts to publicly funded science and research.

items.description: A 76-year-old woman was left "crying buckets" after being sexually assaulted in a Mid North Coast aged care home, a court hears. Her attacker has now been jailed for 12 years.

items.title: French Resistant fighter historian Marc Bloch join the Pantheon greats
items.description: He was a leading historian of the 20th-century, and co-founded French academic journal 'Les Annales' with Lucien Febvre, and was also a soldier in both World Wars. Historian Marc Bloch was tortured and executed by the Gestapo in 1944, and will enter the Pantheon on June 23 in a rare tribute reserved for France's most revered historical figures.

items.title: Sky glows red as storms sweep Russia
items.description: Eyewitness video showed the skies turning a dramatic shade of red over Miass, a city in Russia’s Chelyabinsk, region on Sunday. It comes as local authorities warned of heavy rain and thunderstorms, following downpours that have swept several Russian regions over the past week.

items.description: Iran has contacted Indian refiners to sell its oil after the US temporarily lifted sanctions on Tehran. Meanwhile, an Indian startup founder is going to head Meta's WhatsApp. Follow DW for more.

items.description: In Ethiopia’s Ura refugee settlement, High Commissioner Barham Salih urged greater support for inclusive policies that enable refugees to rebuild their lives alongside hosts.

France's 3-0 win sees them book their spot in the knockout round with a game to spare, while Lionel Messi has broken the record for the most goals scored at the World Cup, with a total of 18 goals since 2006.

items.description: Tasmanian poultry producers are ramping up biosecurity measures, as they prepare for the arrival of a deadly strain of bird flu. The state government has stressed that it's well prepared, but says euthanasia is "definitely possible" if there's an outbreak in commercial poultry.

The milestone comes one day after Prime Minister Keir Starmer's tearful resignation. Also, the internet goes crazy for "hot podium guy", a handsome sound engineer who sets up the lectern in front of 10 Downing Street for each big announcement. Indian leader Narendra Modi inaugurates three new Indian-made naval vessels in a move seeking to counter China's growing influence. Finally, a new statue in honour of Lionel Messi is ridiculed as a "Temu" of Messi statues!

items.description: Republicans and Democrats have both employed tactics to boost weaker rivals ahead of US midterm elections.

items.description: She shunned feminine beauty ideals, was bisexual and a political radical, all while being brutally honest in her art. A major Frida Kahlo exhibition opens at London's Tate Modern.

items.title: Eiffel Tower closes early due to heatwave
items.description: The French capital's Eiffel Tower, one of the most visited tourist attractions in the world, has closed early on June 23 because of exceptionally high temperatures. This comes as Paris struggles to cope with a historic heatwave, and residents try to adapt in a city that is not made to withstand such extreme weather. FRANCE 24's Caroline Baum is at the Eiffel Tower and spoke with some of its last visitors for the day.

items.title: What the US and Iran agreed – and disagreed – on first day of talks
items.description: Leaders from Iran and the US have lauded progress on day one of Switzerland talks but disagree about crucial aspects.

items.title: Tunisian inflation grows, driven up by a rise in food prices
items.description: In Tunisia, inflation topped 5 percent last month, driven by a significant rise in food prices. One cause is supply chain issues and middlemen hiking prices. The crisis is exacerbated by continuing high unemployment rates, especially in marginalized regions far from the capital and the coast. Protests these days often feature slogans about the high cost of living. FRANCE 24's Lilia Blaise and Hamdi Tlili report.

items.title: In Mexico, the World Cup comes at a price for fans
items.description: The passion for football in Mexico is high,.but so too are the prices. A World Cup ticket can cost hundreds of euros, while a ticket for a match in Mexico normally costs around 40 euros. The price of food has also increased. For the majority of Mexicans, these high prices make the World Cup unaffordable, sometimes even overshadowing the excitement of the tournament.

items.title: Two dead in car crash in Tasmania's south
items.description: Two vehicles collided at Old Beach in Tasmania's south earlier today. The two occupants of one of the vehicles have died at the scene, while the other driver is uninjured.

items.title: Senior Albanese minister clashes with Pocock over top-secret AI copyright claim
items.description: Federal politician David Pocock has clashed with a senior Albanese government minister over a whistleblower claim that federal cabinet is considering plans to let AI companies train on Australian copyright material.

items.title: Trump says repairs to algae-plagued Reflecting Pool will begin immediately
items.description: Despite a multi-million dollar renovation, the Washington DC landmark has peeling paint and algae. Trump claims vandals are partly to blame.

items.description: During his chairmanship, Greenspan was celebrated as possibly the best central banker in history. But later, his reputation was tarnished by the worst financial crisis since the Great Depression.

items.description: Iran's foreign ministry says it made "no new commitments" on nuclear inspections after talks in Switzerland.

items.title: Messi smashes World Cup goal record, Mbappé close behind
items.description: Forwards are delivering at the FIFA World Cup. Argentina's Lionel Messi has become the top scorer in the history of the competition, closely followed by France's Kylian Mbappé. Also highly noticed, Norwegian Erling Haaland, who scored a brace against Senegal. Besides Messi, Mbappé and Haaland, football enthusiasts have their eyes on England's Harry Kane, who scored two goals in his opening match, and was the top scorer at the 2018 tournament.

items.title: French heatwave proves deadly as country struggles to adapt
items.description: As France experienced its hottest day yet in its latest heatwave, two children, aged 2 and 4 were found dead inside a parked car in the country's south. It comes after three elderly people died in their homes from heat, according to authorities. Temperatures were forecast to reach up to 43 degrees Celsius across the territory on June 22 and some 39 million people are now under "red alert" warnings for extreme heat.

items.title: 'No one in the UK seems happy over Brexit': Reporter Clovis Casali
items.description: As the UK marks a decade since the Brexit vote that took it out of the European Union, our reporter who's been back to gauge the mood in the UK says no one seems happy with the result. To mark the anniversary, FRANCE 24 filmed a special edition of our Revisited programme, talking to some of those most affected by the decision. Clovis Casali says he found that those who voted to remain are still unhappy that the UK is now no longer an EU member, while those who voted to leave say politicians let them down after the referendum and the hard Brexit they wanted never happened. He spoke to us in Perspective.

items.title: In South Korea, AI chip workers become top marriage prospects
items.description: In South Korea, the AI frenzy has sent the value of the country's top chipmakers skyrocketing. Korea's Samsung Electronics and its chief rival SK Hynix have been trading places as the world's most valuable producer of memory chips. Following some strident union organizing at the firms, their workers have become some of the country's highest-paid - with some performance bonuses at Samsung's chip unit set to top 400,000 dollars this year. That has not only made jobs at the companies highly sought-after: it's also made their employees highly sought-after as marriage prospects.

items.title: Ten years on, the UK's workers take stock of Brexit
items.description: Ten years on from the Brexit referendum, how has the UK's departure from the European Union impacted the country and its economy? A BBC report said the UK economy had taken a six-percent hit from Brexit, according to analysis of internal Bank of England data. Raw GDP numbers published by the OECD suggest that the UK performed broadly in line with other advanced economies in the years immediately following the 2016 referendum, but started lagging behind the US and Canada since the early 2020s. FRANCE 24's Clovis Casali went to ask British workers.

items.title: Alessandro Circati chose Australia over Italy. His dad approves
items.description: Alessandro Circati's decision to play for the Socceroos raised eyebrows within his family in Italy, but his father says they now realise that, deep down, he's not Italian.

items.title: British PM Rishi Sunak avoids wipeout in key local elections
items.description: By-elections were seen as an indicator of the two main parties' prospects at a time when voters are struggling with high inflation, strikes and rising mortgage rates.

items.title: Manuel Adorni asset declaration shows wealth grew 45-fold in 2024
items.description: Cabinet chief’s documentation attempts to explain an astronomical rise in his net worth. His legal strategy and mounting complications in Congress. Leer más

items.title: Undav heroics help Germany beat Ivory Coast
items.description: In Germany's second 2026 World Cup game, Deniz Undav helped Germany to a comeback win against Ivory Coast in Toronto.

items.title: Why was JD Vance in a luxury Swiss resort for talks with Iran?

items.title: Why is Bulgaria threatening to veto EU's Russia sanctions?
items.description: Bulgarian PM Rumen Radev has vowed to veto the EU's latest sanctions against Russia because they include Russian Orthodox Patriarch Kirill and former Lukoil president Vagit Alekperov. What's behind his opposition?

items.title: Argentina MSCI review holds key to foreign investment surge
items.description: MSCI Inc will decide next week whether to set Argentina on the road back to membership in global stock indexes. Leer más

items.title: French mid-sized firms adopt AI but see few gains, survey shows
EU ban on BPA chemical in food packaging takes effect
items.description: From July 2026 onwards, BPA will no longer be permitted in EU food packaging. The industrial chemical is known to interfere with the human hormonal system.

items.title: From the sublime to the ridiculous
items.description: In the past, countless ministers have been sacrificed to protect a President but now presidential popularity is being laid on the line to save a minister. Leer más

items.title: Eight killed in Kebbi farmer-herder clash
items.description: The Kebbi State Police Command said the violence followed a dispute over alleged destruction of farmland by cattle. The post Eight killed in Kebbi farmer-herder clash appeared first on Premium Times Nigeria .

items.title: SA pushes for AI data centres as industry says it's not asking for subsidies
items.description: The South Australian government is pushing for the state to become home to billions of dollars' worth of artificial intelligence data centres, but says it will seek to regulate their water and energy use.

items.title: Iran says card-based banking hit by cyberattack on three lenders

items.description: In this episode, we look at the resignation of U.K. Prime Minister Keir Starmer amid mounting political pressure, the deadly ammonia gas leak at a seafood processing unit in Tamil Nadu’s Tiruvallur district, and more.

items.title: World Cup exploits of Maradona and Messi have Argentina fans in raptures
items.description: Fans buzzing after Lionel Messi bags another two goals in Argentina's second World Cup 2026 match. Leer más

items.title: Inequality is elementary to Germany's school system
items.description: Children's paths through Germany's school system are often determined before their first day of kindergarten, according to the national report on education. An elementary school in Bonn is offering a new model for kids.

items.title: Trump allies defend him to Israelis anxious over Iran deal
items.description: The U.S.-Israeli relationship has been on a roller coaster, from the ​early confidence they shared after their joint attack on Iran to public disagreements between Donald Trump and Prime Minister Benjamin Netanyahu over how ⁠to end the four-month-old war

items.title: Kenyan minister accused of role in recruiting civilians to fight for Russia
items.description: Erastus Mundia left Kenya on a work programme his government had arranged with Russia. He died on the battlefield in Ukraine, and his family holds Labour Minister Alfred Mutua responsible

items.title: Germany's media rocked by AI scandal
items.description: Two German outlets were forced to delete articles that used undisclosed AI. Many fear that reliance on the technology will damage the credibility of German media.

items.title: Shooting at Northern California library kills 2; suspect in custody
items.description: The incident this evening was obviously very sad, traumatic for a lot of people; very traumatic for our community, said Billy Aldridge, the city's chief of police

items.title: NT Labor leader defends office culture in 'unique' political workplace
items.description: Northern Territory Opposition Leader Selena Uibo has defended her handling of workplace misconduct allegations in her office, saying it is a "highly-charged political environment that is unique and unlike most workplaces".

items.title: Yen hovers near 40-year low against dollar
items.description: Japanese Finance Minister Satsuki Katayama's conversation with US Treasury Secretary Scott Bessent on Monday helped the yen recover from a low of 161.93 per dollar, just short of the 161.96 level last seen in December 1996.

items.title: The man who knew, but didn't say: Alan Greenspan (1926-2026)
items.description: Alan Greenspan’s reputation as head of the US Federal Reserve was legendary — not least because of his reputation for convoluted statements. However, the 2008/9 financial crisis took a toll on his standing.

items.title: Britain left the EU 10 years ago. Its politics has been an unruly mess
items.description: Brexit fractured the European Union, and broke British politics. A decade on, millions of voters have deserted the two big parties for alternatives.

items.title: World Cup blues in Messi and Milei’s Argentina
items.description: The World Cup provides partial reprieve for nearly six weeks, especially if Messi & Co are in it for a deep run. Politics, though, continues its course, and Adorni is coming up to the chopping block. Leer más

items.title: At least 13 killed and dozens injured after Qatar gas explosion
items.description: The city's main liquified natural gas (LNG) processing site suffered a "technical accident" in the Ras Laffan industrial zone.

items.title: 75 years of Germany's Goethe-Institut
items.description: For 75 years, the Goethe-Institut has stood for cultural exchange, education and partnerships around the globe — even under challenging political conditions.

items.title: New dollar-cash videos put Insaurralde case back into spotlight

items.title: Iran says Strait of Hormuz closed again after Israeli attacks on Lebanon
items.description: Tehran said deadly Israeli strikes in Lebanon are a breach of its agreement with the United States to end the war.

items.title: 'Ridiculous and disrespectful': Inquiry into NDIS cuts extended
items.description: Disability advocates welcome an extension to a Senate inquiry probing a bill paving the way for the biggest-ever cuts to the NDIS.

items.title: French celebrate win against Iraq despite two-hour delay caused by storm
items.description: The weather in the US tried to put a stop to the France-Iraq game at the World Cup on June 22... and it did: the game had to be halted for two hours as a huge thunderstorm hit in Philadelphia, where it was being held. France ended up winning 3-0, with two of them coming from French superstar Kylian Mbappé. But nothing could deter French fans from celebrating.

items.title: Heatwave: France records hottest-ever night
items.description: France saw its hottest night since records began in 1947 from Monday to Tuesday, with temperatures across the country averaging 21.6C after darkness fell, according to the country’s weather agency. In Paris, some without air conditioning at home spent the night in parks to get relief from the heat.

items.title: Uganda conservationists increase efforts to protect mountain gorillas
items.description: As Uganda battles a deadly outbreak of the Ebola virus, conservationists are increasing efforts to protect the country's mountain gorillas. The authorities say they need tourists to continue to visit the endangered primates or th

items.title: BBC sees destroyed villages in Israeli-occupied southern Lebanon
items.description: Travelling with a humanitarian convoy, BBC's Hugo Bachega has been given rare access to a part of Lebanon under Israeli occupation.

items.title: France bans alcohol during Fete de la Musique amid heat wave
items.description: France is taking additional measures as millions of people gear up for Fete de la Musique under punishing temperatures.

items.title: Afghan Taliban clamp down on women, smartphones, protests
items.description: The Taliban are tightening the screws on women's clothing, shooting at protesters and ordering even their own officials to stop using smartphones. Behind the regime's omnipresent control, there are signs of deep anxiety.

items.title: Fed hike could push yen to 165 per dollar, ex-BOJ policymaker says
Would you choose to take a 22-hour non-stop flight?
items.description: The BBC asked Sydney locals if they would take the newly announced, longest ever commercial flight from Sydney to London.

items.title: Fact check: AI fakes around the World Cup spread political narratives
items.description: Keir Starmer in a Croatia jersey. An Iranian protest against a US strike. Calls to arrest Brazil's President Lula. Adolf Hitler in the German fan section — all supposedly seen at the World Cup. But none of it is real.

items.title: Confirmed Ebola cases rise to 1003, including 254 deaths, DRC says
items.description: The number of confirmed Ebola cases in the Democratic Republic of the Congo has risen to 1003, including 254 deaths, authorities in the country said on Sunday.

items.title: Spain: PM's wife must face corruption trial, judge rules
items.description: A court in Madrid ruled that there is sufficient evidence against Begona Gomez, the wife of Prime Minister Pedro Sanchez. The 55-year-old university director is banned from leaving the country.

items.title: Should the US impose a teen social media ban like the UK?
items.description: The UK is enforcing a social media ban for children under the age of 16. The BBC asked Americans of different ages if they thought it was a good idea.

items.title: Ten years on, 'jury still out' on Brexit although early signs of damage show
items.description: Ten years ago, the UK voted to leave the European Union with promises that Brexit would help the country economically. But a recent study suggests the economy actually took a six-percent hit. Support for Brexit has decreased: younger voters, who would have not been able to vote at the time, today mostly support a return to the EU if given the chance. FRANCE 24's Douglas Herbert tells us more.

items.title: Lalo Schifrin’s final symphony, Rod Schejtman named Argentine cultural ambassador
items.description: Rod Schejtman, the composer Lalo Schifrin designated as his artistic heir, now carries Argentina’s national brand to the world’s concert halls. Leer más

items.title: Milei to stand by his man as calls for Adorni’s sacking grow

items.title: Former Kenyan justice minister blocked from entering Uganda, lawyers' body says
items.description: Martha Karua is a lawyer representing detained Ugandan opposition leader Kizza Besigye who is on trial for treason.

items.title: Brexit: Discord in the UK music industry, 10 years on
items.description: Ten years after the referendum that shook the United Kingdom, what impact has Brexit had on one of its greatest exports: music? From major festivals in the UK to the grassroots venues in Europe determined to host British bands, FRANCE 24's Olivia Salazar-Winspear sets out to meet the people keeping the music scene alive.

items.title: New Mariners owner snubs women's side, coach told services not needed
items.description: A new owner has been confirmed for struggling A-League club the Central Coast Mariners, but it has only bought the men's team and the club's academy, leaving the future of the women's team in doubt.

items.title: World Cup awaits Argentina's Julián Álvarez, the striker worth – at least – 150 million euros

items.description: Of those held, 185 are suspected of membership in several far-left organisations designated as terrorist groups by Ankara

items.title: Vondrousova, ex-Wimbledon champion, insists she has ‘never doped’
items.description: Vondrousova, who won Wimbledon in 2023, was issued a four-year ban from tennis after she refused to submit a ⁠sample.

items.title: EV demand powers Europe car market in May, Chinese rivals expand share
Largest ever cocaine bust in Australia after police raid underground bunker
items.description: Police seized 2.7 tonnes of cocaine worth an estimated A$816m after searching a property in western Sydney.

items.title: Gunman kills an officer at a Montreal hotel before being shot dead, police say
items.description: Police say a suspect armed with a long gun opened fire at a Montreal hotel, killing a police officer before officers returned fire, killing him.

items.title: Finland may approve Tesla's supervised self-driving software before EU vote

items.title: India: What’s driving the intense pressure on students?
items.description: The reported deaths of aspiring medical students in India have exposed the immense pressures of the country's high-stakes examination system and a worsening mental-health crisis.
